entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00131
notes: The file is 125,838 bytes at the recorded CME submodule commit.
It contains 2 DIV1, 5 DIV2, 50 DIV3, 52 PB, 157 P, 33 NOTE, and one TABLE element. The 5 DIV2 and 50 DIV3 counts describe the encoding structure, not physical manuscript units.

The TEMPHEAD records a later review dated 2016-08-30, including removal of a fragment and checks of page-break completeness; it does not establish the original keying date or prove that every printed page milestone is present.

entities.description: A public structured digital text encoding a discontinuous selection from Raine's 1890 printed edition.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00131
notes: The exact encoded carrier selection is the title page plus printed pp. 1–29, 35–52, and 58–63.
It omits section III pp. 30–34, section V pp. 53–57, all of section VII, the glossary appendix, the index, and other preliminaries, paratext, and apparatus.
The XML declares TEXT/@LANG as enm; the selection also contains Latin headings or formulae and modern English editorial matter.

The PB sequence has two unnumbered milestones, numbered 2–29, 35–50, and 58–63. Textual comparison shows that section IV's printed pp. 51–52 follow PB 50 and are present without corresponding milestones.

rights.basis: The U-M item and collection pages state that the materials are in the public domain.
rights.component: U-M digital encoding and encoded edition text

bibliographic.citation: James Raine Jr., ed., A Volume of English Miscellanies Illustrating the History and Language of the Northern Counties of England, Publications of the Surtees Society 85, for the year 1888 (Durham: published for the Society by Andrews & Co. and others, 1890), ix, 100 pp.
bibliographic.series: Publications of the Surtees Society, volume LXXXV
bibliographic.title: A Volume of English Miscellanies Illustrating the History and Language of the Northern Counties of England

entities.description: A seven-section documentary miscellany with a glossary appendix and index, compiled and edited from northern English civic, manorial, and literary sources.
entities.label: James Raine Jr.'s 1890 A Volume of English Miscellanies
notes: The series leaf says the volume is for 1888, but the title page gives 1890 and the council order is dated 3 December 1889.

The title page does not name an editor. The council order assigns preparation to the Society's Secretary, the preface's autobiographical statement identifies its writer as James Raine Jr., and the Folger catalog explicitly credits James Raine as editor.

The complete volume has seven numbered sections: I pp. 1–10, II pp. 11–22, III pp. 22–34, IV pp. 35–52, V pp. 53–57, VI pp. 58–63, VII pp. 64–75, followed by appendix/glossary pp. 77–93 and index pp. 94–100.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1890; U-M marks the encoded materials public domain and Wikimedia Commons marks the corresponding Internet Archive volume public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1890 edition text and editorial matter

notes: Date-compatible candidate children are U DDLO/21/27 (1471–72), /28 (1472–73), /29 (1473–74), /30 (1475–76), /32 (1477), /35 (1479), and /40 (1483–85). They are candidates, not asserted item-level matches.

next_steps: Request or inspect Explore York's legacy finding aids and concordances.
Compare every Raine incipit and explicit legacy locator against the current volumes rather than assuming numeric continuity.
Record the crosswalk at passage level while preserving both locator systems.
openQuestions.question: How do Raine's A/Y, B/Y, and Roman-numbered House Book locators map to current foliation and child shelfmarks, including the relationship between A.Y. 255 and fols. 264v.36–267v.6?

next_steps: Ask Hull History Centre for item-level descriptions, endorsements, or legacy concordances for U DDLO/21/27–30, /32, /35, and /40.
Compare incipits, jury place names, regnal dates, and membrane sequences against complete roll images.
Do not assign an exact child solely from Raine's sometimes inconsistent marginal dates.
openQuestions.question: Which U DDLO/21 court-roll components and membranes contain the eight CME-encoded section III presentments, and how should their edition dates be reconciled?

next_steps: Contact North Yorkshire Archives, the Fitzwilliam (Malton) Estate, and repositories holding Wentworth-Fitzwilliam papers with Raine's exact title, incipit, and provenance statement.
Search estate transfer, sale, and deposit records rather than assuming the post-1593 ZPB collection contains the roll.
If located, request a complete-object surrogate and establish whether Raine printed the whole roll or a selection.
openQuestions.question: Where is the fifteenth-century New Malton customs roll reported in Earl Fitzwilliam's possession in 1890, and does it survive under a current shelfmark?

next_steps: Inspect surviving keying specifications, proofsheets, production logs, or U-M project documentation.
Treat the current text and phrase boundaries as evidence of inclusion and omission; do not manufacture PB 51 or PB 52 or restore omitted text without provenance.
Record any future repair as a new encoding intervention rather than retroactively changing source lineage.
openQuestions.question: Why does CME00131 stop section III at printed p. 29 and omit section V, while retaining section IV pp. 51–52 without PB milestones despite the 2016 review note saying PB completeness was checked?

reviewNotes: The encoded scope is stated by printed pages, not merely as sections I–IV and VI: title page plus pp. 1–29, 35–52, and 58–63, with the explicit exclusions recorded in the summary and selection practice.

The XML's PB sequence cannot alone delimit the included text. Printed pp. 51–52 of section IV are textually present after PB 50, although PB 51 and PB 52 are absent; phrase and content comparison establishes that boundary.

The current York references Y/COU/1/4/1, Y/COU/1/4/2, and Y/COU/1/1 are supported at volume or series level. Raine's legacy A/Y, B/Y, and Roman-numbered House Book locators have not been silently converted into current component shelfmarks.

The Selby relation is assigned to U DDLO/21 at aggregate level, with dated child candidates recorded as candidates rather than exact excerpt matches. The distinct U DWE catalog was cached and consulted as a rejected alternative, not conflated with the source series.

Complete-facsimile negatives refer only to public web access checked on 2026-07-13. Explore York and Hull History Centre provide onsite consultation and mediated copying routes, but reproduction and publication conditions remain separate from access.
The New Malton customs witness is modeled from Raine's 1890 statement. No current repository, shelfmark, survival confirmation, or public surrogate has been established.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00131 XML is a University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of a discontinuous selection from James Raine Jr.'s 1890 Surtees Society volume, not a transcription of one manuscript and not the complete printed volume. It contains the edition title page and printed pp. 1–29, 35–52, and 58–63. Thus it contains sections I and II, only pp. 22–29 of section III, section IV, and section VI; it omits section III's pp. 30–34, all of section V (pp. 53–57), all of section VII, the glossary appendix, the index, and the other preliminaries, paratext, and apparatus. Section IV's text on printed pp. 51–52 is present even though the XML has no PB milestones for 51 or 52. The selected texts derive from several York civic record groups, Selby manorial court rolls, and a New Malton customs roll reported in 1890; their manuscript, edition, encoding, artifact, and modern scan layers remain distinct. A complete Internet Archive access PDF and its complete supporting derivatives are cached, as are complete Hull History Centre catalog PDFs used to assess the Selby witness. No public complete manuscript facsimile was found on 2026-07-13, and the current location of the New Malton roll remains unresolved.

catalogingSubject.label: A Volume of English Miscellanies Illustrating the History and Language of the Northern Counties of England: CME00131 documentary selection
catalogingSubject.scope_note: A composite, discontinuous selection from James Raine Jr.'s 1890 modern documentary miscellany, represented by five top-level encoded groups: section I complete (pp. 1–10), section II complete (pp. 11–22), only pp. 22–29 of section III, section IV complete (pp. 35–52), and section VI complete as a printed section (pp. 58–63), plus the edition title page. The selection is not one medieval document, not one manuscript, and not the whole 1890 carrier: it omits section III pp. 30–34, section V pp. 53–57, all of section VII, the glossary appendix, the index, and other preliminaries, paratext, and apparatus. The medieval documentary groups, archival witnesses, Raine's edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, and Internet Archive scan remain distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.label: I. Proceedings in a Case of Adulteration at York
notes: Complete relative to carrier section I. The relation between legacy A.Y. 255 and modern fols. 264v.36–267v.6 remains a locator-system question.
contentParts.summary: A 1428 York civic investigation and adjudication concerning false osmund iron, adulterated tin and alum, craft searchers, trade fraud, and the punishment and negotiated restoration of John Lyllyng.

contentParts.label: II. Verdicts of the Searchers in the City of York
notes: Complete relative to carrier section II. item_count and metrics count this top-level encoded documentary group as one extent unit; its twenty-one nested containers are Raine/U-M editorial units, not a verified physical-document count.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-one editorial document containers of verdicts and awards by York craft searchers concerning building lines, walls, gutters, drainage, privies, encroachments, fixtures, and shared property obligations from 1417 to 1501.

contentParts.label: III. Presentments at the Courts of the Abbot of Selby, encoded portion

locators.value: U DDLO/21/27–30, /32, /35, and /40; exact mapping unresolved

notes: Partial relative to carrier section III: CME stops cleanly at the bottom of printed p. 29 and omits pp. 30–34. item_count and metrics count this partial top-level group as one extent unit; the eight nested containers remain described in the summary. The candidate roll children are date-compatible only and are not asserted one-to-one matches.
contentParts.summary: Eight encoded editorial units of jury presentments associated with the Selby manorial court rolls, dated or assigned approximately 1472–1483, concerning local offences, customary duties, maintenance, and community regulation.

contentParts.label: IV. Certificates of English Parentage and Birth
notes: Complete relative to carrier section IV. item_count and metrics count this top-level encoded documentary group as one extent unit; its twenty-one nested containers remain described in the summary. Printed pp. 51–52 are textually present after PB 50, although the encoding has no PB 51 or PB 52; the phrase/content boundary, not the PB count, establishes completeness.
contentParts.summary: Twenty-one editorial containers of certificates, letters, attestations, and related records documenting the English birth and parentage of people charged with being Scots, dated 1476–1497 and enrolled in York civic records.

contentParts.label: VI. Customs and Liberties of the Burgesses of New Malton

notes: Complete relative to carrier section VI. Completeness relative to the original roll is unknown, and the witness's current location and survival are unresolved.
contentParts.summary: A fifteenth-century prose text setting out offices, procedures, customs, obligations, and liberties of the New Malton burgesses.

notes: The Surtees Society council ordered the Secretary to prepare the volume on 3 December 1889; the title page gives 1890. The series leaf's phrase 'for the year 1888' is an assigned Society year, not a demonstrated compilation or publication date.

First publication of Raine's compiled miscellany

extent.description: Five encoded top-level documentary groups, corresponding to carrier sections I, II, partial III, IV, and VI, all in prose. They contain one direct prose unit in I, twenty-one nested editorial units in II, eight of the carrier section III units, twenty-one nested editorial units in IV, and one direct prose unit in VI. The resulting 52 terminal encoding units are editorial/structural metrics, not a physical-document count. The unencoded carrier sections V and VII are not counted, nor are the glossary appendix, index, or other paratext.

formStatements.description: Five prose documentary groups comprising proceedings, verdicts, court presentments, certificates or letters, and borough customs.

scope.description: York civic proceedings, building verdicts, and enrolled certificates
Vernacular proceedings and testimony
Vernacular verdicts and awards
Vernacular jury presentments
Vernacular certificates, attestations, and letters
Vernacular borough customs text
Latin openings, headings, dating formulae, names, and documentary formulae embedded in chiefly vernacular records

languageStatements.label: English
notes: Modern editorial English is distinct from the Middle English documentary text. This facet covers only editorial passages retained by CME00131, not Raine's unencoded preface, glossary apparatus, index, or other omitted carrier matter.

scope.description: The encoded edition title page, section headings, marginal dates, source labels, and editorial notes retained with the five documentary groups

notes: The five content parts, not the seven carrier sections, define encoded extent. Section III is partial; sections I, II, IV, and VI are complete relative to their printed section boundaries.
The 50 DIV3 containers plus the two directly encoded prose groups are editorial and encoding units, not a verified total of independent medieval documents or physical objects.
James Raine Jr. is recorded as editor, compiler, transcriber, and commentator of the 1890 miscellany, never as author of its medieval civic, manorial, certificate, or borough records.
Section IV printed pp. 51–52 is present after PB 50 even though the XML lacks PB 51 and PB 52. Completeness is based on phrase/content comparison with the carrier, not milestone count alone.

No complete public facsimile was found for the York, Selby, or New Malton manuscript sources on 2026-07-13. The cached Internet Archive PDF is a complete scan of Raine's printed carrier, not of the manuscripts.

The research exposed no reusable modeling lesson beyond the existing skill guidance on separating layers, caching complete carrier objects, preserving legacy/current locator uncertainty, and comparing phrase boundaries when milestone sequences are incomplete; no shared skill file was edited by this research worktree.

next_steps: Inspect complete images and endorsements for candidate children U DDLO/21/27–30, /32, /35, and /40.
Match incipits, jury places, regnal dates, and membrane order rather than relying only on marginal dates.

next_steps: Contact North Yorkshire Archives, the Fitzwilliam (Malton) Estate, and repositories holding Wentworth-Fitzwilliam papers with Raine's exact title, incipit, and provenance.
Search deposit, transfer, and sale records without assuming ZPB contains the roll.
If located, request the complete object and compare it with Raine pp. 58–63 before calling the edition transcription complete.

next_steps: Inspect U-M keying specifications, proofsheets, and production logs.
Retain the current phrase-based selection boundary and do not restore text or manufacture PB milestones without provenance.
Record any future restoration or PB repair as a new encoding intervention.

subjects.label: Building survey, property boundaries, and urban drainage

summaries.value: This composite selection brings together five distinct groups of late medieval northern English records. A 1428 York proceeding investigates adulterated iron, tin, and other trade goods; twenty-one York searchers' verdicts regulate encroachments, boundaries, gutters, walls, and building obligations from 1417 to 1501; eight selected Selby manorial jury presentments report local offences and obligations in the 1470s and 1480s; twenty-one York-enrolled certificates address the English parentage and birth of people accused of being Scots from 1476 to 1497; and a fifteenth-century text sets out the customs and liberties of New Malton burgesses. Raine's modern editorial selection is valuable for civic government, craft regulation, urban property, manorial jurisdiction, identity, documentary practice, and borough custom, but it does not make these records one medieval work.

CME00131 contains the 1890 edition title page and five top-level body groups: section I, one account on pp. 1–10; section II, twenty-one editorial document containers on pp. 11–22; the first eight containers of section III on pp. 22–29; section IV, twenty-one containers on pp. 35–52; and section VI, one customs text on pp. 58–63. Section IV pp. 51–52 is textually present after PB 50 even though PB 51 and PB 52 are absent. The encoding omits section III pp. 30–34, section V pp. 53–57, all of section VII, the glossary appendix, the index, and other preliminaries, paratext, and apparatus.

This record catalogs the five-group CME documentary selection as a composite unit inherited from Raine's modern miscellany. It does not assign James Raine Jr. authorship of the historical records, treat the selection as one manuscript, count the seven carrier sections as encoded extent, or infer exact manuscript components from legacy locators and dates. The five top-level groups are the primary extent units. Their 50 nested DIV3 containers and two directly encoded prose groups yield 52 edition/encoding terminal units, but these are not a verified count of independent physical documents. Each group therefore carries its own date, place, language, witness, and completeness scope.

notes: The suffix identifies the cataloging unit as the CME selection rather than the complete 1890 carrier.

A 1428 York proceeding investigates adulterated iron, tin, and other trade goods; twenty-one York searchers' verdicts regulate encroachments, boundaries, gutters, walls, and building obligations from 1417 to 1501; eight selected Selby manorial jury presentments report local offences and obligations in the 1470s and 1480s; twenty-one York-enrolled certificates address the English parentage and birth of people accused of being Scots from 1476 to 1497; and a fifteenth-century text sets out the customs and liberties of New Malton burgesses. Raine's modern editorial selection is valuable for civic government, craft regulation, urban property, manorial jurisdiction, identity, documentary practice, and borough custom, but it does not make these records one medieval work.
